scenario: Raix work session - extend the tool-calling pipeline

map_quality.rationale: All required pieces are named with concrete paths: ChatCompletion, FunctionDispatch, FunctionToolAdapter, the message adapters, and the full MCP subtree (tool.rb, sse_client.rb, stdio_client.rb). The agent could pick any entry point and start with targeted lookups. Minor gap: the answer never states these are concerns/mixins *included into a host class*, so the agent doesn't immediately know the composition boundary without one more lookup.

specificity.rationale: Paths are fully qualified throughout (lib/raix/chat_completion.rb, lib/raix/mcp/sse_client.rb, etc.) and module names are exact; two entries even carry line numbers (lib/raix.rb:23-24). No vague references like 'the chat code'.

justification.rationale: The answer names and summarizes each module's role but never explains WHY the design is a composable mixin set included à la carte into a host class. The architectural rationale (behavior added on demand by inclusion) is absent; the agent gets what, not why.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents every piece with full confidence and flags nothing for verification. How the concerns compose into a host class, whether FunctionDispatch and ChatCompletion are always included together, or where the dispatch loop re-enters are all left unqualified.

steps.step: Trace a tool-call round-trip through the chat loop

map_quality.rationale: The answer gives a complete nine-step ordered trace with file:line at every layer — entry (chat_completion.rb:73, function_dispatch.rb:114), model call (:147/:340), tool detection (:160-161), limit check (:162-177), dispatch (:180-187/:297), transcript append (function_dispatch.rb:80-101), recursion (:198-209), and final text (:221-235); an agent could locate and change any of these layers with only small, targeted follow-up reads.

specificity.rationale: Every piece is named exactly — dispatch_tool_function, max_tool_calls, stop_tool_calls_and_respond!, ruby_llm_request, FunctionToolAdapter.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm — each tied to a concrete file:line rather than described vaguely; the agent has a precise address for each seam.

justification.rationale: The answer explains how results re-enter — the DSL method pushes an assistant+tool pair into the transcript (function_dispatch.rb:80-101), then the loop reads the updated transcript and recurses if the last message is not plain text — which conveys the mechanism; it does not explain the design rationale for this recursive rather than iterative loop shape, so a reader understands what happens but not why it was built this way.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer names the branch points (max_tool_calls cutoff, stop_tool_calls_and_respond! short-circuit, the FunctionDispatch super-wrapping) but presents them as confident assertions rather than flagging them as 'verify before editing'; an agent acting on this answer might not know to probe the stop_tool_calls_and_respond! path or the multiple-tool-calls-per-response iteration before committing a change.

steps.step: Map how functions are declared and exposed to the model

map_quality.rationale: Every leg of the pipeline is named with file:line: DSL at function_dispatch.rb:46, storage at :27/:47-48, tool wrapping at :126-130, schema conversion via FunctionToolAdapter at function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57/create_tool_from_function :6-48, filtering at chat_completion.rb:303-313, and MCP filtering at mcp.rb:85-93 — an agent could add a new declared capability from this map alone with only small follow-up reads.

specificity.rationale: Every piece is named exactly — FunctionDispatch.function, @functions, attr_reader :functions, FunctionDispatch#tools, FunctionToolAdapter.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm, create_tool_from_function, filtered_tools, UndeclaredToolError — all with precise line ranges; nothing is left as 'the function code'.

justification.rationale: The answer describes mechanics thoroughly (what each piece does and how it connects) but never explains WHY the design uses a class-level DSL converted to schema — the key design intent that host classes declare tools declaratively so behavior is added a la carte is absent; the mention of define_method at :67 hints at runtime dispatch but the rationale is not stated.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer names the strict-mode defaults and UndeclaredToolError guard but never explicitly flags where the agent should verify before acting — no calls out on schema format subtleties, the name-matching in filtered_tools, or whether MCP filtering composes correctly with local filtering; it reads as uniformly confident.

steps.step: Map how the conversation is adapted before it is sent

map_quality.rationale: All three rubric-required adapters (transcript_adapter.rb, message_adapters/base.rb, multimodal_content_adapter.rb) are mapped with file:line, instantiation points in chat_completion.rb are cited, and the 4-stage flow (flatten → normalize → multimodal translate → RubyLLM replay) gives the agent a clear entry point. The only gap is that full lib/raix/ path prefixes are omitted, a minor friction for a follow-up read.

specificity.rationale: Every adapter is named by file with per-method line ranges (e.g., 'base.rb:17-27', 'multimodal_content_adapter.rb:51-61', 'chat_completion.rb:340-372') rather than vague labels; method names (add_message_from_hash, normalize_message_format, MultimodalContentAdapter.translate) are exact enough for targeted lookups.

justification.rationale: Provider-shape normalization is explained for the Anthropic claude-3 cache-control path ('multipart conversion at :42-45') and multimodal content ('converts OpenAI image_url arrays … into RubyLLM::Content'), but the TranscriptAdapter's bridging role (why abbreviated :system/:user/:assistant hashes exist at all, why flattening is needed) is described as mechanism without stating the underlying why.

uncertainty.rationale: Conditional branches (Anthropic vs. other providers, base64 vs. URL images, abbreviated vs. raw hash forms) are described as facts rather than flagged as 'verify before acting'; the answer never signals where the agent could get the adaptation wrong or where line numbers might drift, leaving it to act without an explicit verification prompt.

steps.step: Map how external tools plug into the same dispatch

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces the full MCP path with file:line throughout: Tool representation at mcp/tool.rb:11, both transport clients with specific method lines, the DSL at mcp.rb:41/54/70, and the critical convergence point where `function(local_name, ...)` (mcp.rb:108) funnels external tools into the same `self.class.functions`/`FunctionToolAdapter`/`dispatch_tool_function` path as local ones. An agent could open a PR from this alone with only small targeted lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every piece is named by concrete file:line — `mcp/sse_client.rb:47`, `mcp/stdio_client.rb:26`, `mcp.rb:70-162`, `mcp.rb:109-113`, `mcp.rb:120-157` — with no vague references to 'the MCP code'; method names (`call_tool`, `unique_key`, `coerce_arguments`) are tied to exact locations.

justification.rationale: The answer explains WHY the normalization exists — 'so MCP tools flow through `self.class.functions`, `FunctionToolAdapter`, and `dispatch_tool_function` exactly like local ones' — making the one-calling-path design intent explicit, not just a list of files; the `include FunctionDispatch` step at mcp.rb:96 is called out as the structural hook that achieves this.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer describes where the external path diverges (transport clients, schema sourced from server, `coerce_arguments`) but does not flag these as places the agent should verify before acting; it reads as confident throughout, so the agent won't know to double-check transport error handling, schema format edge cases, or the `only`/`except` filter interaction before committing.

steps.step: Find every site that depends on the tool-call contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer covers all six required areas (chat loop, declaration+schema, conversation adapters, external MCP, specs) with concrete file:line citations throughout — e.g., `dispatch_tool_function :297-299`, `filtered_tools :303-313`, `mcp/sse_client.rb tools/call_tool/unique_key :32,:47,:86`, VCR cassettes; an agent could safely navigate a contract change from this list alone with only small targeted lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every site is named by concrete file and line range rather than vague category — e.g., `continuation/recursion + stop handling :194-218`, `create_tool_from_function :6-48`, `tool message handling :85-97,:110-111`; no hand-wavy 'update the tool code' phrasing.

justification.rationale: Section headers ('Chat loop / dispatch', 'Declaration & schema', 'External tools') gesture at WHY each group is affected, but within each section the answer only lists line ranges without explaining the causal role each site plays (e.g., why transcript_adapter.rb must change when the contract changes, or why filtered_tools is a critical gate); the rubric's 'runs vs declares vs converts vs adapts vs external vs tests' dimension is implicit at best.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the list as complete with no flags for what static reading can miss — no mention that mixin-only sites are invisible until a host class includes them, no caution about MCP transport edge cases, and the VCR cassette note is a bare listing rather than an explicit 'verify these before committing' warning.

map_quality.rationale: All four areas are mapped with file:line throughout — dispatch loop ':180-187', DSL ':46-107', adapters ':17-47', MCP proxy ':99-157' — plus a concrete spec list including VCR cassettes and the run command; a teammate could open the PR from this map with only minor targeted lookups to confirm context around each range.

specificity.rationale: Every change point is tied to an exact file and line range (e.g., 'dispatch_tool_function :297-299', 'create_tool_from_function :6-48', 'mcp/sse_client.rb:32,47,86') with no vague references to 'the tool code'; the JSON summary reinforces the prose with a redundant but consistent second pass.

justification.rationale: Each file is labeled with its pipeline role ('primary site for detection', 'schema sent to model', 'must mirror any change to the local contract') but the answer names roles rather than explaining WHY the architecture requires it — e.g., it doesn't explain that mcp.rb must mirror because it reuses FunctionDispatch via include, or why the transcript pair in function_dispatch.rb :80-101 is the feedback re-entry contract.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags conditional inclusions ('only if extending content shapes', 'only if the call_tool result contract changes') but does not call out the harder uncertainty zones — whether the loop re-enters recursively or iteratively, which VCR cassettes need regeneration vs. editing, or where schema format mismatches between provider adapters could silently break things.
